title: The Old Woman in the Wood

content.text: The forest was not like other forests.
The trees stood too close together, their gnarled roots twisting above the earth like skeletal fingers.
The air smelled of damp rot and something older, something that had been buried long before the first axe ever split wood.
And the silence—oh, the silence was wrong.
No birds, no rustling leaves, just the occasional whisper of wind that sounded almost like a voice, just out of reach.
Elise had been walking for hours, though she could not say how many.
The path behind her had vanished the moment she stepped off it, swallowed by the undergrowth as if it had never existed.
She clutched the small bundle of firewood to her chest, the only thing her mistress had allowed her to take when she sent her into the woods to gather more.
"Don't dawdle," the woman had snapped, her lips curling like dried parchment.
"And don't you dare come back empty-handed."  Elise had not intended to get lost.
But the forest had other plans.
The first sign that something was amiss came when the shadows began to move.
Not with the wind, not with any living thing—just shifting, stretching, as if the darkness itself had taken a breath.
Elise quickened her pace, her boots sinking into the soft, black earth. Then she heard the laughter.
It was a dry, cracking sound, like branches snapping in a fire.
It came from everywhere and nowhere, wrapping around her like a shroud.
Elise broke into a run, her breath coming in ragged gasps.
The trees seemed to lean in, their bark peeling back in places to reveal something slick and wet beneath.
She did not look. She did not dare. Then she saw the cottage.
It squatted in a small clearing, its walls sagging, its thatched roof rotting.
The door hung ajar, as if inviting her inside. Elise hesitated.
The firewood in her arms suddenly felt heavier, as though it were trying to pull her forward.
She took a step. Then another. The laughter came again, closer this time. It was coming from the cottage.
Elise turned to flee, but the trees had closed ranks behind her. The path was gone.
The only way out was through the door. She stepped inside.
The air was thick with the scent of mildew and something metallic, like old blood.
The walls pulsed faintly, as if breathing.
In the corner, a figure sat hunched over a spinning wheel, her back to Elise.
The woman—if it was a woman—was impossibly old, her skin stretched tight over bone, her fingers like twisted roots.
The wheel spun of its own accord, thread unraveling from nothing, weaving itself into nothing.
"You're late," the old woman croaked, her voice like dead leaves scraping stone.
"I've been waiting."  Elise tried to speak, but her throat was too dry.
The old woman turned slowly, her face a ruin of wrinkles and hollows, her eyes two black pits that seemed to swallow the light.
"You'll do nicely," she whispered. "Yes, very nicely."  The spinning wheel stopped. The thread snapped.
Elise stumbled back, but the door had vanished. The walls pressed in, the cottage shrinking around her.
The old woman rose, her joints popping like dry kindling, and reached for Elise with fingers that ended in needle-like claws.
Then—just as the first claw grazed Elise's sleeve—there was a sound.
A deep, resonant chime, like a bell tolling from far away. The old woman froze.
Her face twisted in fury, her mouth stretching too wide, too many teeth glinting in the dim light.
"Too soon," she hissed. "Too soon!"  The chime came again, louder this time, vibrating through the floorboards.
The old woman shrieked, her form flickering like a candle flame in the wind.
The walls of the cottage groaned, the roof splitting apart as golden light poured in, blinding and warm.
Elise shielded her eyes, and when she lowered her hands, the cottage was gone.
She stood in the middle of the forest once more, but the trees were just trees now, their bark rough and ordinary beneath her fingertips.
The path lay before her, clear and unobstructed.
The air smelled of pine and earth, no trace of rot or decay.
And in her arms, the bundle of firewood was heavier than before.
When she looked down, she saw that the sticks had transformed—no longer mere wood, but gold, gleaming softly in the afternoon light.
Elise did not look back. She ran, her heart pounding, until she reached the edge of the forest.
The mistress stood there, her arms crossed, her foot tapping impatiently.
But when Elise held out the golden firewood, the woman's scowl faltered. Her eyes widened, then narrowed with greed.
Elise said nothing.
She simply turned and walked away, leaving the mistress to stare after her, the golden sticks clutched in trembling hands.
The forest remained quiet behind her, as if it had never been anything more than trees and shadows.
But Elise knew better.
She knew what lurked in the depths of the wood, and she knew, too, that it would not forget her.
But for now, she was safe.
For now, the chime had rung, and the old woman had been forced back into whatever dark corner she had crawled from.
And for now, that was enough.
